pattern: Count substrings with consonant-count <= k that also contain all five vowels using two independent, forward-only pointers -- one bounding how far left you can shrink before losing a vowel, the other bounding how far left you can extend before exceeding the consonant budget -- then take the exactly-k answer as atMost(k) - atMost(k-1). The much larger word bound here is what forces the two-pointer O(n) version instead of the quadratic scan.
hints: "Exactly k consonants" is the difference of two easier counts: (substrings with all vowels and at most k consonants) minus (substrings with all vowels and at most k-1 consonants).

For a fixed right endpoint, the set of valid left endpoints for 'at most k consonants, all vowels present' is a contiguous range -- one pointer finds how far left you must go to have all 5 vowels, another finds how far left you can go before consonants exceed k.
Maintain both pointers as monotonic, forward-only sliding windows as the right endpoint advances -- that keeps the whole count computation linear in the length of word.

edge_inputs.description: exactly the five vowels, zero consonants allowed
word never contains all five vowels
minimum word length, exactly the vowels
k = 0 but there are stray consonants everywhere
repeated vowels with a variable run of consonants between qualifying windows
k at its maximum allowed value for this word

stress.description: near-linear-limit random word, proving the O(n) two-pointer scales
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(5, min(size, 150000))
    word = ''.join(rng.choice('aeiouqr') for _ in range(n))
    k = rng.randint(0, n - 5)
    return [word, k]
